channel.title: Modern Classics
channel.description: Classic public-domain texts, retold in contemporary English. New retellings as they are finished.

item.title: Cato's Letters — John Trenchard and Thomas Gordon
item.description: Eighteen selected letters on liberty, power, and free speech — the anonymous newspaper columns that taught the American colonies how to argue for revolution.

item.title: Roman Lives — Plutarch
item.description: The fall of the Roman Republic in five lives — Caesar, Cato, Cicero, Brutus, and Antony — by the ancient world's greatest biographer.

item.title: Nicomachean Ethics — Aristotle
item.description: The original inquiry into the good life: happiness as the goal, virtue as trained habit aiming at the mean, two books on friendship, and the case for the thinking life.

item.title: Tusculan Disputations — Cicero
item.description: Five days of dialogues at a country villa on the things that scare us most — death, pain, grief, and the passions — ending with the case that virtue alone is enough for happiness.

item.title: The Autobiography of Benjamin Franklin — Benjamin Franklin
item.description: The original American life story: a runaway apprentice becomes printer, scientist, and statesman — told with the dry wit of a man who brags and then winks at the brag.

item.title: On Duties — Cicero
item.description: Cicero's last book, written as a letter to his son: what the right requires, what advantage tempts, and why nothing dishonorable is ever truly useful.

item.title: The Memorabilia — Xenophon
item.description: Socrates remembered by his other student: not the ironist of Plato's dialogues but the practical teacher — on friendship, work, self-control, and how to be useful to your city.

item.title: The Way to Wealth — Benjamin Franklin
item.description: Twenty-five years of Poor Richard's proverbs stitched into one speech about work, thrift, and debt — delivered to a crowd that applauds every word and then ignores it.

item.title: The Enchiridion — Epictetus
item.description: The Stoic pocket manual: fifty-one short drills for keeping your head — sort what is up to you from what isn't, and want what actually happens.

item.title: Selected Essays — Michel de Montaigne
item.description: The invention of the essay: a retired French magistrate turns his pen on himself — on friendship, fear, cannibals, thumbs, and death — and finds the whole human condition there.

item.title: Utopia — Thomas More
item.description: The book that named a genre: a sailor describes the strange island commonwealth of Utopia, where property is common, gold is worthless, and everything about European society is quietly turned on its head.

item.title: True History — Lucian of Samosata
item.description: The original science fiction: a voyage to the moon, war between the sun and moon empires, and life inside a whale — narrated by an author who promises that every word is a lie.

item.title: The Social Contract — Jean-Jacques Rousseau
item.description: "Man is born free, and everywhere he is in chains" — the book that asked what could make political authority legitimate, and helped ignite a revolution.

item.title: Leviathan — Thomas Hobbes
item.description: Written in the shadow of civil war: why life without government is solitary, poor, nasty, brutish, and short — and why we trade liberty for a sovereign to escape it.

item.title: The Histories — Herodotus
item.description: The first history book ever written: the Persian Wars, plus every marvel, custom, and gossip-worthy story its insatiably curious author picked up along the way.

item.title: The Prince — Niccolò Machiavelli
item.description: The infamous handbook of power politics: how princes win states, keep them, and why it is safer to be feared than loved.

item.title: Candide, or Optimism — Voltaire
item.description: A wide-eyed young man raised to believe this is the best of all possible worlds gets kicked around the entire globe testing the theory.

item.title: The Decameron — Giovanni Boccaccio
item.description: Ten young Florentines flee the Black Death and pass ten days telling a hundred stories — bawdy, tragic, scandalous, and very much alive.

item.title: Dialogues — Plato
item.description: Socrates in conversation: the trial, the prison cell, the drinking party, and the relentless questions that got him executed.

item.title: De la démocratie en Amérique — Alexis de Tocqueville
item.description: The second volume of the great French study of America: how equality reshapes not just politics but thought, feeling, art, ambition — and how democracies can drift into a soft new despotism.

item.title: Two Treatises of Government — John Locke
item.description: The philosophical foundation of liberal government: natural rights, property earned by labor, rule by consent, and the people's right to dissolve a government that betrays its trust.

item.title: An Essay on the Principle of Population — Thomas Robert Malthus
item.description: The most alarming arithmetic ever published: population grows geometrically, food arithmetically, and the gap is settled by misery — an argument that has haunted optimists since 1798.

item.title: On the Origin of Species — Charles Darwin
item.description: One long argument that changed biology forever: variation, inheritance, and the struggle for existence add up to natural selection — and to the common descent of all living things.

item.title: Philosophical Works — René Descartes
item.description: "I think, therefore I am": the philosophical works that doubted everything — the senses, the world, even mathematics — to find the one certainty that could rebuild knowledge.

item.title: Commentaries on the Gallic War — Julius Caesar
item.description: A general's own account of conquering Gaul: eight years of campaigns, written in the third person by the man who fought them — part war report, part campaign ad.

item.title: History of the Peloponnesian War — Thucydides
item.description: The war that broke Greece, told by a general who fought in it: plague, massacre, the Melian dialogue, and the doomed expedition to Sicily — history written as a possession for all time.

item.title: Flatland: A Romance of Many Dimensions — Edwin A. Abbott
item.description: A Square living in a two-dimensional world is visited by a Sphere from the third dimension, and nothing about his flat life survives the encounter.

item.title: Common Sense — Thomas Paine
item.description: The pamphlet that talked a continent into independence: a plain-language demolition of monarchy and the case for an American republic.

item.title: The Federalist Papers — Alexander Hamilton, James Madison, and John Jay
item.description: The eighty-five newspaper essays that sold a skeptical public on the United States Constitution — and still explain how it is supposed to work.

item.title: Democracy in America — Alexis de Tocqueville
item.description: A young French aristocrat tours Jacksonian America and writes the sharpest account of democracy — its energy, its conformity, and its future — ever produced by a visitor.

item.title: The Wealth of Nations — Adam Smith
item.description: The book that invented economics: the division of labor, the invisible hand, and a sweeping case that nations grow rich through freedom of trade, not hoards of gold.

item.title: Progress and Poverty — Henry George
item.description: Why does poverty deepen as wealth explodes? A self-taught San Francisco printer's answer — land rent — became the best-selling economics book of the nineteenth century.

item.title: Meditations — Marcus Aurelius
item.description: The private notebook of a Roman emperor: reminders to himself about how to stay calm, do his duty, and face death without fear.

item.title: Moral Letters to Lucilius — Seneca
item.description: All 124 letters from a Stoic: an old philosopher writes his friend a daily course in living — time, friendship, fear, fortune, and how to die well.
